title: Nike Art Gallery
description: The world's largest private art gallery, spanning four floors of breathtaking Nigerian and African art — paintings, textiles, sculptures, and installations curated by the legendary Chief Nike Davies-Okundaye.
long_description:
Nike Art Gallery in Lekki is not just an art gallery — it's a temple to Nigerian creativity. Founded by Chief Nike Davies-Okundaye, one of Nigeria's most celebrated textile artists, this four-storey building houses over 8,000 works of art from emerging and established Nigerian artists.

The ground floor features large-scale paintings and sculptures. Upper floors showcase traditional Adire and Batik textiles, beadwork, woodcarvings, and contemporary installations. The rooftop offers a stunning view of Lekki.

Entry is free, and the gallery operates as both an exhibition space and a working art studio where you can watch artists at work. It's one of the most photographed spots in Lagos and an essential stop for any cultural visitor.

title: Lekki Conservation Centre
description: Home to Africa's longest canopy walkway — a 401-metre elevated boardwalk through lush tropical wetland. Spot monkeys, crocodiles, and exotic birds in this unexpected nature sanctuary within Lagos city.
long_description:
Hidden within the urban sprawl of Lekki, this 78-hectare nature reserve is a sanctuary of biodiversity and calm. The star attraction is the 401-metre canopy walkway, suspended high above the forest floor, offering breathtaking views of the wetland ecosystem below.

The reserve is home to monkeys, crocodiles, various snake species, and over 100 species of birds. Nature trails wind through mangrove and swamp forest, and observation platforms let you spot wildlife in their natural habitat.

It's one of Lagos's most popular attractions for both tourists and locals seeking an escape from the city's intensity. Early morning visits offer the best wildlife sighting opportunities.

title: New Afrika Shrine
description: The spiritual home of Afrobeat music, founded by the legendary Fela Kuti and now run by his sons Femi and Seun. Live music every Sunday night — an unmissable Lagos experience.
long_description:
The New Afrika Shrine is where the spirit of Fela Anikulapo Kuti lives on. Located in Ikeja, this open-air venue is the successor to Fela's original Afrika Shrine and is now managed by his sons Femi and Seun Kuti.

Every Sunday night features live Afrobeat performances — Femi Kuti often performs with his band, delivering electrifying shows that blend music, politics, and spirituality in the true Fela tradition.

The venue is raw, authentic, and unapologetic — concrete floors, open-air seating, cold beer, and the best live music you'll hear anywhere in Africa. The annual Felabration festival (October) is a week-long celebration of Fela's legacy that draws fans from around the world.

This is not a polished tourist attraction — it's a living, breathing cultural institution. Come with an open mind and leave with a changed soul.

title: Freedom Park
description: A former colonial-era prison transformed into Lagos's premier open-air cultural centre. Live music, art exhibitions, theatre, and events in a beautifully restored heritage site on Lagos Island.
long_description:
Freedom Park sits on the site of the old Broad Street Prison, a colonial-era facility that once held political prisoners and ordinary citizens alike. After its decommissioning, the site was transformed into a stunning open-air recreation and cultural centre.

The park preserves elements of the original prison architecture while creating modern spaces for art, music, and performance. Regular events include live jazz, spoken word, book readings, and art exhibitions.

It's a peaceful oasis in the chaos of Lagos Island, and a powerful symbol of transformation — from a place of confinement to a space of creative freedom.

title: Terra Kulture
description: Lagos's premier arts and culture centre — a hub for Nigerian theatre, art exhibitions, language classes, and cultural events on Victoria Island.
long_description:
Terra Kulture is Lagos's most important cultural institution after the National Theatre. Founded by Bolanle Austen-Peters, it hosts world-class Nigerian theatre productions, art exhibitions, and cultural programming.

The centre includes an art gallery, bookshop, restaurant, and performance theatre. Regular productions showcase the best of Nigerian storytelling, from contemporary dramas to adaptations of Yoruba folklore.

The on-site restaurant serves excellent Nigerian cuisine, making it a perfect spot to combine culture with food. Language classes in Yoruba and other Nigerian languages are also available.

title: Badagry Heritage Museum
description: A powerful and moving museum documenting the transatlantic slave trade's impact on West Africa. Walk the 'Point of No Return' — the route enslaved Africans took to the ships.
long_description:
Badagry, about 1.5 hours west of Lagos, was one of the major ports of the transatlantic slave trade in West Africa. The Heritage Museum and surrounding sites tell this devastating but essential story with dignity and historical rigour.

Visit the Slave Museum, the First Storey Building in Nigeria (where missionaries established the first school), and walk the solemn 'Point of No Return' path leading to the beach where millions of Africans were shipped into slavery.

The Badagry Heritage Museum houses artifacts, chains, and documents from the slave trade era. A boat ride across the Badagry Creek to the Slave Market and the Spirit of Badagry monument completes the experience.

This is essential history for any visitor — especially those from the diaspora seeking to understand the origins of the African dispersion.

title: Olumo Rock
description: A majestic granite outcrop that served as a natural fortress for the Egba people during 19th-century wars. Climb ancient steps, explore sacred caves, and enjoy panoramic views of Abeokuta.
long_description:
Olumo Rock is the defining landmark of Abeokuta — quite literally, the city's name means 'Under the Rock.' This massive granite formation served as a strategic fortress during the inter-tribal wars of the 19th century, providing shelter and protection for the Egba people.

Today, visitors can climb the rock via ancient steps carved into the granite or take a modern elevator installed for accessibility. Along the way, you'll pass through caves with traditional shrines, see ancient grinding holes used by women to prepare food during the wars, and encounter a 137-year-old sacred tree.

From the summit, you get a sweeping 360-degree panorama of Abeokuta and the surrounding countryside. At the base, a cultural centre and market sell local crafts and Adire textiles.

Olumo Rock is the single most important day trip from Lagos for understanding Yoruba history and the resilience of the Egba people.

title: Adire Textile Market, Itoku
description: The world capital of Adire — Yoruba resist-dyed indigo cloth. Watch master craftswomen create stunning patterns using centuries-old techniques and buy authentic textiles directly from the makers.
long_description:
Itoku in Abeokuta is the global epicenter of Adire — the Yoruba art of resist-dyeing cotton fabric using indigo. This ancient craft, recognized by UNESCO as an intangible cultural heritage, produces stunning blue-and-white patterns that are instantly recognizable.

At Itoku Market, you can watch the entire process: women (called Alaro) draw patterns on fabric using cassava paste (Adire Eleko) or tie threads and raffia for resist-dye patterns (Adire Oniko). The fabrics are then dipped in pots of natural indigo dye.

Buying Adire directly from the craftswomen here is the most authentic way to own these textiles — and prices are a fraction of what you'd pay in Lagos boutiques or international markets.

This is a must-visit for fashion enthusiasts, textile lovers, and anyone interested in living African craft traditions.

title: Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site — one of the last remaining primary high forests in southern Nigeria, dotted with shrines, sculptures, and sanctuaries dedicated to the Yoruba goddess Osun.
long_description:
The Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove is one of Africa's most important spiritual sites and a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2005. This dense forest along the banks of the Osun River is the spiritual home of Osun — the Yoruba goddess of fertility, beauty, love, and the river.

Within the grove, over 40 shrines, sculptures, and artworks — many created by Austrian-Nigerian artist Susanne Wenger (Adunni Olorisha) and her collaborators — blend seamlessly with the ancient trees and winding paths.

The most iconic sight is the monumental Osun sculpture at the river's edge. The grove pulses with spiritual energy — it's a place of worship, not just tourism, and visitors are expected to show respect.

Every August, the Osun-Osogbo Festival draws hundreds of thousands of devotees and spectators for a two-week celebration culminating in the Arugba's procession to the river.

For anyone interested in Yoruba spirituality, the diaspora religions (Candomblé, Santería, Vodou), or simply in experiencing one of Africa's most powerful sacred landscapes, this grove is unmissable.

title: Ooni of Ife's Palace
description: The seat of one of Africa's most ancient and revered monarchies. The Ooni of Ife is regarded as the spiritual leader of all Yoruba people worldwide — a living connection to the origin of Yoruba civilization.
long_description:
Ile-Ife is the sacred cradle of Yoruba civilization — the place where, according to Yoruba cosmology, the god Obatala descended from heaven to create the earth. The Ooni of Ife sits on what many consider the oldest throne in Africa.

The current Ooni, Oba Adeyeye Enitan Ogunwusi (Ojaja II), is a modern monarch who balances tradition with contemporary engagement. His palace complex is a blend of traditional Yoruba architecture and modern additions.

While the inner chambers are restricted, visitors can view the palace grounds, the traditional court areas, and attend public ceremonies if timing aligns. The Ooni is known for receiving guests and cultural tourists.

Combine a palace visit with the nearby National Museum of Ile-Ife, which houses the world-famous Ife bronze and terracotta heads — some of the most sophisticated artworks ever produced in pre-colonial Africa.

title: National Museum, Ile-Ife
description: Home to the world-famous Ife bronze and terracotta heads — masterpieces of pre-colonial African art that challenged Western assumptions about African civilization.
long_description:
The National Museum in Ile-Ife houses one of Africa's most significant art collections. The Ife bronze heads, dating from the 12th-15th centuries, are among the most naturalistic sculptures ever produced — their sophistication stunned European scholars when they were first encountered, as they challenged prevailing racist assumptions about African artistic capability.

The museum displays bronze heads, terracotta figures, stone carvings, and other artifacts spanning centuries of Ife civilization. Each piece tells a story about Yoruba royal courts, religious practices, and artistic excellence.

This is one of Nigeria's most important museums and a must-visit for anyone interested in African art history. Many of the most famous Ife bronzes are in British and European museums, making the pieces in this collection all the more precious.

title: Idanre Hills
description: A breathtaking cluster of hills rising dramatically from the surrounding plains. Climb 640 steps to reach the old town settlement — complete with a palace, court, and thunder god shrine — all perched at the summit.
long_description:
Idanre Hills (Oke Idanre) is one of Nigeria's most dramatic natural landmarks. This cluster of high, smooth-sided hills rises from the surrounding plain in Ondo State (near the Osun border), creating a landscape that feels almost otherworldly.

The main attraction is the climb — 640 steps take you to the hilltop settlement where the Idanre people lived until 1928. At the summit, you'll find the ruins of the old town, including the Oba's Palace, the old court, a shrine to the thunder god Sango, and the mysterious 'Agboogun Footprint' believed to be that of the town's legendary founder.

The views from the top are spectacular — endless rolling green hills and valleys stretching to the horizon. The climb is moderately challenging and takes about 30 minutes at a comfortable pace.

Idanre Hills has been submitted to UNESCO's Tentative World Heritage List and represents one of Southwest Nigeria's most impressive natural and cultural sites.
